reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The page opens with a clear, accurate definition of a Stacktape web service and puts pricing context near the top.
The load-balancing section gives readers a real decision between `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer` instead of dumping properties.
The basic example is realistic, copyable, and close to the top, which fits the first-time-user audience.
The page covers the main operational questions this audience actually has: domains, CDN, compute, scaling, health checks, private subnets, logging, and remote sessions.
The FAQ is strong: it includes Stacktape-specific questions, AWS behavior questions, and comparison questions.
problems: The EC2 warm-pool discussion relies on `enableWarmPool`, but that property is not documented in the provided `web-services.d.ts` source. Either source it explicitly for WebService or remove the warm-pool paragraph and example.

The CDN section is thin relative to the rest of the page and contains more decision guidance than the provided source directly supports. It would be stronger with a small config example or a shorter source-grounded note that points readers to the CDN page.
The page does not mention service alarms at all, even though `alarms` and `disabledGlobalAlarms` are exposed on `WebServiceProps` in the source. That leaves a visible gap in the operational story.
The CDN and EC2 sections read more advanced than the rest of the page and may be harder for a first-time Stacktape user to parse quickly than the other sections.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source the `enableWarmPool` Web Service guidance. The current draft depends on a property that is not grounded in the provided WebService source.
Tighten the CDN section so every claim is directly grounded in the provided source, and either add a small config example or shorten it to a concise pointer to the CDN resource page.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief alarms note for `alarms` and `disabledGlobalAlarms`, or explicitly say the page is leaving alerting to the API reference.
If you want to mirror the sibling page more closely, add an `<Info>` callout for the pricing context instead of leaving that detail in prose.
Consider trimming a little from the advanced sections so the top half of the page stays focused on the most common path: load balancing, domains, and compute.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong early decision guidance: the page clearly positions web-service against Lambda function, private-service, worker-service, multi-container-workload, and batch job.
The load balancing section is practical and correctly distinguishes http-api-gateway, application-load-balancer, and network-load-balancer by pricing model, feature support, and protocol fit.
The page correctly preserves the important firewall distinction between top-level useFirewall on an Application Load Balancer and cdn.useFirewall for CloudFront/CDN scope.
Stacktape config examples use class-based TypeScript inside <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/> with the correct TypeScript tab label and no hand-authored YAML.
The FAQ covers Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison questions, and it has exactly 10 Q&A pairs.
problems: MDX component palette drift from the example page: the example uses both <Info> and <Warning>, while this draft only uses <Warning>.

The page says Stacktape creates DNS records and TLS certificates for custom domains, but the provided source says disabling DNS creation still skips DNS records while Stacktape provisions TLS; the draft is mostly right but could more explicitly state the Route53 requirement only applies to Stacktape-managed DNS record creation.

The Basic example says the default traffic path gets a public HTTPS URL without defining a separate load balancer resource. That is consistent with the WebService nested httpApiGateway shape, but the draft would be safer if it named the default as an implicitly managed HTTP API Gateway rather than leaving readers to infer what Stacktape creates.
The CDN section is useful for decision-making but has no concrete config example, even though cdn is an important opt-in feature on this resource page.

The page covers alarms only indirectly through the API reference. Since WebServiceProps has alarms and disabledGlobalAlarms, production readers may expect at least a short pointer to monitoring/alarms behavior.
mandatoryFixes: Match the example's MDX component palette. Add or remove callouts to match the example's per-section pattern.
optionalImprovements: Add a small CDN <CodeBlock> example if the source for CdnConfiguration is available in the generation context; otherwise keep the section decision-focused and link to the CDN page as drafted.

Clarify custom domain wording: Stacktape provisions a certificate and normally creates DNS records when the domain has a Route53 hosted zone; set disableDnsRecordCreation when DNS is managed elsewhere.
In Basic example, say the default web-service load balancing mode creates/manages an HTTP API Gateway path for the public HTTPS endpoint.

Add a short Observability or Alarms paragraph near Logging that says web-service alarms can target HTTP API Gateway or Application Load Balancer metrics, and that disabledGlobalAlarms excludes named global alarms, without enumerating every alarm type.
Consider adding an <Info> callout for NAT Gateway cost or pricing context to align better with the reviewed sibling page style.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Strong answer-first opening and a clear early "When to use" / "When NOT to use" split.
Good union coverage for traffic modes, compute engines, packaging modes, and health-check variants.
The page is practical: it includes realistic config examples, comparison tables, and a 10-question FAQ that covers Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison queries.
The structure is easy to scan and mostly matches the sibling resource-page pattern.
problems: Several sections rely on configuration details that are not grounded in the provided source files, especially `Scaling` (`minInstances`, `maxInstances`, scaling-policy thresholds), `Gradual deployments` (strategy names and timing), `Compute resources` (`enableWarmPool` behavior), and `EFS storage` (mount-field details).
The ALB health-check defaults are written without units in one place (`interval 5`, `timeout 4`), which makes the timing ambiguous.
`Domains and CORS` mixes two different concerns, and the CORS guidance is too brief relative to the rest of the page.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow unsupported option names, defaults, and timing claims in `Scaling`, `Gradual deployments`, `Compute resources`, and `EFS storage` unless the exact source types are available; where the source is silent, say so instead of filling in details.
State health-check units explicitly wherever you mention default intervals or timeouts, e.g. `5 seconds` and `4 seconds`.
optionalImprovements: Add a short monitoring note for `alarms` and `disabledGlobalAlarms`, or link readers to the alarm docs if you want to keep this page focused.
Split `Domains and CORS` into a domain subsection and a short CORS note so readers can find the load-balancing dependency faster.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the WebService source for core behavior, load balancing modes, domains, CORS, WAF scoping, NAT, pricing context, and ApiReference usage. The main publication risks are several nested configuration examples and operational claims that are not grounded in the provided source files.

issues.statement: The Compute resources section states that ARM tasks are configured with `architecture: 'arm64'`, shows that property in a WebService `resources` example, and says EC2 mode exposes `enableWarmPool` with weekly instance refreshes for OS patching.
issues.reason: The provided WebService/SimpleServiceContainer source establishes that web services have a `resources: ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` property, that Fargate is the default, and that EC2 mode is selected with `instanceTypes`. The provided source does not define an `architecture` property, an `enableWarmPool` property, or weekly EC2 instance refresh behavior. The pricing source supports the ARM discount, but not the Stacktape configuration property used to select ARM.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced Fargate-vs-EC2 guidance and the ARM pricing note only if you can cite the `ContainerWorkloadResourcesConfig` source that defines `architecture`. Remove `enableWarmPool` and weekly refresh wording unless a provided type or validator explicitly supports them.

evidence.quote:
Two compute engines:
   * - **Fargate** (default): Serverless — just specify `cpu` and `memory`.
   * - **EC2**: Specify `instanceTypes` for more control and potentially lower cost.
Prices are us-east-1 list price; ARM (Graviton) tasks bill ~20% less than x86.

issues.statement: The Scaling section shows `minInstances`, `maxInstances`, `scalingPolicy.keepAvgCpuUtilizationUnder`, and `scalingPolicy.keepAvgMemoryUtilizationUnder`, then explains those exact fields as Stacktape web-service scaling controls.
issues.reason: The provided source confirms that a web service has a `scaling?: ContainerWorkloadScaling` property and that traffic is distributed across running containers, but it does not provide the nested `ContainerWorkloadScaling` definition or the specific property names used in the example. These are Stacktape-specific config fields, so they need source grounding.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite the source file that defines `ContainerWorkloadScaling` with these fields, or narrow the section to the sourced statement that Stacktape supports auto-scaling for web services without showing unsupported nested properties.

evidence.quote:
Auto-scaling: add/remove container instances based on demand.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Traffic is automatically distributed across all running containers.

issues.statement: The EFS storage section shows `volumeMounts.properties.efsFilesystemName`, `mountPath`, and `rootDirectory`, and states that `rootDirectory` defaults to `/` when omitted.
issues.reason: The provided source supports that web services can define `volumeMounts?: ContainerEfsMount[]` and that EFS data persists, but it does not include the `ContainerEfsMount` definition or the claimed `rootDirectory` default. The default value is a specific Stacktape behavior and should not be asserted without the nested type or validator source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the `rootDirectory` default claim and avoid the detailed nested EFS example unless the `ContainerEfsMount` source is provided. A safer sourced version can say that web services support persistent EFS volume mounts through `volumeMounts`.

evidence.quote:
Persistent EFS volumes shared across containers and restarts.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Data stored in EFS volumes persists even when containers are replaced.
   * Multiple containers can mount the same volume. All data is encrypted in transit.

issues.statement: The Gradual deployments section uses `deployment.strategy: 'Canary10Percent5Minutes'` and says the deployment feature includes validation-hook behavior.
issues.reason: The provided source supports that web-service `deployment` enables gradual traffic shifting and requires `application-load-balancer`, and it names canary, linear, and all-at-once at a high level. It does not provide the `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` shape, the literal strategy value `Canary10Percent5Minutes`, or validation-hook behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the literal strategy example with sourced high-level guidance, or add the source defining `ContainerWorkloadDeploymentConfig` and its allowed strategies. Remove validation-hook wording unless it is present in the provided deployment source.

evidence.quote:
Gradual traffic shifting for safe deployments (canary, linear, or all-at-once).
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Requires `loadBalancing` type `application-load-balancer`.

issues.statement: The Health checks section shows `internalHealthCheck.healthCheckCommand`, `intervalSeconds`, `timeoutSeconds`, `retries`, and `startPeriodSeconds`.
issues.reason: The provided source supports the existence and purpose of `internalHealthCheck?: ContainerHealthCheck`, but it does not include the nested `ContainerHealthCheck` definition or the specific property names used in the example. Because this is a copyable Stacktape config example, unsupported nested fields are risky.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite the `ContainerHealthCheck` type source that defines these exact fields, or keep the section conceptual and avoid a detailed config example.

evidence.quote:
Health check that auto-replaces unhealthy containers.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * If a container fails the health check, it's terminated and replaced automatically.

issues.statement: The Logging section says to use the `logging` property when you need to disable logging or change container log behavior.
issues.reason: The provided source confirms that `logging?: ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging` exists and that stdout/stderr are sent to CloudWatch with 90-day retention. It does not show that logging can be disabled, because the nested logging type is not included in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced default logging behavior and link to log forwarding. Remove “disable logging” unless the `ContainerWorkloadContainerLogging` source is provided and includes that capability.

evidence.quote: Container output (`stdout`/`stderr`) is automatically sent to CloudWatch and retained for 90 days.

positiveFindings: `<ApiReference definitionName="WebServiceProps" />` matches the page metadata type and uses the required `Props` suffix.

The load-balancing mode coverage is well grounded: the draft names all three WebService union variants and preserves the distinct constraints for HTTP API Gateway, Application Load Balancer, and Network Load Balancer.

The draft correctly keeps top-level `useFirewall` separate from `cdn.useFirewall`, matching the source requirement that top-level WAF attachment requires Application Load Balancer while CDN WAF attachment uses CDN scope.
The Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a visible `label: 'TypeScript'` tab and do not hand-author YAML alternatives.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is largely grounded in the provided WebService, shared container, packaging, pricing, and helper type sources. I found one concrete unsupported Stacktape-specific claim that should be corrected before publication.

issues.statement: “Use a [multi-container-workload](/resources/compute/multi-container-workload) instead when multiple containers need independent traffic routing, per-container public integrations, or peer-level lifecycle configuration.”
issues.reason: The provided sources support that web service side containers run as helper containers and that multi-container workload exists as another workload type, but they do not support the specific claim that multi-container workloads provide “per-container public integrations” or “peer-level lifecycle configuration.” The only integration types shown for container workloads are type definitions, not a statement that multi-container workload is the right alternative for per-container public integrations. This could mislead readers about an unstated feature boundary.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to the sourced distinction: use web service side containers when one main public container owns the endpoint and helpers are secondary; use multi-container workload only when you need a workload model centered on multiple containers. Avoid claiming per-container public integrations or peer-level lifecycle behavior unless additional multi-container workload source is provided.

evidence.quote: Helper containers that run alongside the main container.

- **`run-on-init`**: Runs to completion before the main container starts (e.g., database migrations).
   * - **`always-running`**: Runs for the entire lifecycle (e.g., log forwarders, monitoring agents).
   *   Can reach the main container via `localhost`.

positiveFindings: The draft uses the correct `<ApiReference definitionName="WebServiceProps" />` value for the page metadata and includes exactly one API reference.

The load balancing modes, default `http-api-gateway`, ALB-only constraints for `deployment` and top-level `useFirewall`, and the separate `cdn.useFirewall` path are accurately scoped to the provided `WebServiceProps` source.
The pricing context matches the provided pricing source and uses approximate wording for regional variation.
The Stacktape config examples use `defineConfig`, class-based resources, and `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with the required `TypeScript` tab label, with no hand-authored YAML config examples.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 Q&A pairs and mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and choosing/comparison questions.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly complete for WebServiceProps: it covers the main traffic, compute, scaling, packaging, connection, health-check, deployment, networking, logging, session, EFS, and side-container concerns, and it acknowledges the important load-balancing and container-packaging union variants. I found one medium API-completeness gap: the page does not document the explicit `environment` property, which many web-service users will need for runtime configuration.

issues.statement: The draft discusses injected environment variables from `connectTo`, but does not mention the `environment` property for user-defined runtime environment variables.
issues.reason: `environment` is a common web-service configuration path for API keys, feature flags, and runtime settings. Because the page only discusses automatically injected variables, readers may miss where to configure their own variables or secrets. This is not covered by a prose mention, code example, or direct link in the draft outside the generic API reference.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short section or paragraph, likely near “Connecting to other resources,” that names `environment` explicitly and shows or links how to pass static values and secret/directive-backed values into the container. Keep it concise and leave exhaustive syntax to the API reference or directives/secrets docs.

evidence.quote: Environment variables injected into the container at runtime. Use for configuration like API keys, feature flags, or secrets.

positiveFindings: The draft names all `loadBalancing` union variants: `http-api-gateway`, `application-load-balancer`, and `network-load-balancer`, and gives feature/cost tradeoffs for each.
The draft names all container packaging variants: Stacktape image buildpack, custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, Nixpacks, and external buildpack.

The main opt-in feature examples are demonstrative: scaling sets `scaling`, custom domains sets `customDomains`, ALB/NLB traffic examples set `loadBalancing`, side containers set `sideContainers`, EFS sets `volumeMounts`, gradual deployments set `deployment`, and private subnets set `usePrivateSubnetsWithNAT`.
The page uses exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="WebServiceProps" />`, matching the metadata type name.
The draft keeps top-level `useFirewall` and `cdn.useFirewall` constraints scoped separately, avoiding the common nested-property flattening error.
